quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: RRB ALP is the Railway Recruitment Boards’ centralised recruitment process for the Indian Railways Assistant Loco Pilot (ALP) post, rather than a standalone academic qualification.

quick_facts.label: Current recruitment context
quick_facts.value: The most recent detailed notification located is CEN 01/2026. It notified 11,127 provisional ALP vacancies across participating RRBs; the notification states that vacancies may increase or decrease according to Railway Administration requirements.

quick_facts.label: Entry qualification
quick_facts.value: For CEN 01/2026, applicants needed Matriculation/SSLC plus a recognised NCVT/SCVT ITI in specified trades, or a Course Completed Act Apprenticeship in those trades, or a three-year diploma/engineering degree in Mechanical, Electrical, Electronics, Automobile Engineering, or permitted combinations of those disciplines.

quick_facts.label: Selection stages
quick_facts.value: The notified process comprises CBT-1, CBT-2, Computer Based Aptitude Test (CBAT), document verification, and medical examination.

quick_facts.label: Medical standard
quick_facts.value: ALP is an A-1 medical-standard post. The 2026 notice requires 6/6 distant vision in each eye without glasses, specified near vision without glasses, and passing colour, binocular, field, night, and mesopic vision tests; candidates who have undergone LASIK or other refractive-error corrective surgery are ineligible for this post.

quick_facts.label: Pay context
quick_facts.value: In CEN 01/2026, ALP was listed at Level 2 of the 7th CPC pay matrix with initial pay of ₹19,900. This is notification-specific pay context and can change under future government pay rules or recruitment notices.

sections.heading: Purpose and role in the career pathway
paragraphs: RRB ALP is the entry route used by Railway Recruitment Boards to select Assistant Loco Pilots for Indian Railways. It is especially relevant to candidates coming from ITI, NCVT/SCVT trade training, Course Completed Act Apprenticeship, National Apprenticeship Certificate-related pathways, or eligible engineering diploma/degree routes.

A candidate does not receive an ALP job merely by taking the CBTs. The RRB first recommends empanelled candidates; the concerned Railway Zone issues the appointment offer after document verification, medical fitness, and other required checks. Zone or production-unit allocation is subject to merit, the candidate’s preferences, medical standard, and vacancies.
bullets: The latest detailed notice found, CEN 01/2026, listed the post as Assistant Loco Pilot, Pay Level 2, with medical standard A-1.
Vacancy totals are provisional: candidates should treat the individual CEN and its corrigenda as controlling for a specific cycle.

sections.heading: Eligibility: technical qualification, age, and medical fitness
paragraphs: For CEN 01/2026, the core technical routes were: Matriculation/SSLC plus ITI from an NCVT/SCVT-recognised institution in one of the listed trades; Matriculation/SSLC plus Course Completed Act Apprenticeship in those trades; or Matriculation/SSLC plus a three-year diploma in Mechanical, Electrical, Electronics, Automobile Engineering, or combinations of those disciplines. A degree in the same engineering disciplines was acceptable in lieu of the diploma. Applicants awaiting final results were not eligible.

The base age band in that cycle was 18–30 years as on July 1, 2026. The notice provided category-specific upper-age relaxations, including five years for SC/ST and three years for OBC-NCL candidates, subject to the notification’s conditions and required evidence. Age rules must be checked afresh for each CEN.

Medical eligibility is central, not a formality. The ALP post carries A-1 standards. The 2026 notice calls for vision without glasses at the stated thresholds and passing several functional vision tests. It also says that LASIK or another surgical refractive-error correction makes a candidate ineligible for ALP; final fitness is determined by Railway medical authorities.
bullets: Listed ITI/apprenticeship trades include Fitter, Electrician, Instrument Mechanic, Millwright/Maintenance Mechanic, Electronics Mechanic, Mechanic (Motor Vehicle), Wireman, Tractor Mechanic, Mechanic (Diesel), Turner, Machinist, and Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning Mechanic, among others specified in Annexure A.
PwBD candidates were marked “Not Suitable” for the ALP post in the 2026 post-wise parameters.
Candidates should preserve original qualification, category, identity, and other supporting documents: RRB verifies eligibility against originals at document verification.

sections.heading: Selection format and scoring
paragraphs: CBT-1 is a screening test: 75 objective questions in 60 minutes for 75 marks. Its syllabus covers mathematics, mental ability, and general science at roughly Class 10 level. CBT-1 marks are used to shortlist candidates for CBT-2 and do not count in the final panel.

CBT-2 has 175 questions over 2 hours 30 minutes: Part A has 100 questions in 90 minutes, while Part B has 75 questions in 60 minutes. Part A covers mathematics, general intelligence and reasoning, and basic science and engineering. Part B is a qualifying trade test based on Directorate General of Training trade syllabi; every candidate must obtain 35% in Part B. For ITI/trade-apprenticeship candidates, Part B is in the candidate’s own trade.

Candidates who qualify CBT-2 may be shortlisted for CBAT. Every CBAT test battery must be cleared separately with a minimum T-score of 42, irrespective of community. CBAT is offered only in English and Hindi and has no negative marking. Final merit for document verification uses a 50:50 weighting of CBT-2 Part A marks and CBAT scores; appointment remains conditional on document and medical clearance.
bullets: CBT-1 and CBT-2 each apply negative marking of one-third of a mark for every wrong answer.
CBT question papers are available in English, Hindi, and the regional languages listed in the notification; the CBAT is only in English and Hindi.

The 2026 notice states that CBT-2 shortlisting is ordinarily RRB-wise at up to 15 times vacancies, and CBAT shortlisting is ordinarily up to eight times vacancies, subject to the notice’s conditions and RRB discretion.

sections.heading: How to apply and what to monitor
paragraphs: Applications are online-only through the official RRB application route. Candidates may submit only one application to one RRB for a CEN; multiple applications to the same or different RRBs can result in rejection and debarment. The application requires account creation using a personal mobile number and email address; those account details and the chosen RRB cannot later be changed.

For the 2026 cycle, the application window ran from May 15, 2026 through June 14, 2026, with online fee payment due by June 16, 2026 and a paid modification window from June 17 through June 26, 2026. Those dates are past and were specific to CEN 01/2026, not a prediction of future cycles.

The 2026 examination fee was ₹500 for most applicants, with ₹400 refundable after appearing in CBT-1, less bank charges. The fee was ₹250 for SC, ST, ex-servicemen, female, transgender, minority, and EBC candidates, refundable after appearing in CBT-1, less bank charges. Fees, refunds, application requirements, and dates can change in a future CEN.

Candidates should use official RRB sites for city intimation, e-call letters, result notices, and corrigenda. The notification advises Aadhaar or DigiLocker authentication to make identity checking smoother, while stating that alternate-ID applicants may face stricter scrutiny.
bullets: Before submission, check that names and date of birth match the Matriculation/SSLC certificate.
The 2026 process used a live photo capture and required an uploaded signature; SC/ST candidates seeking the applicable travel facility also had to upload the required certificate.

The 2026 online form included a self-screening medical declaration. Candidates reaching CBAT had to bring the resulting report signed by a general physician and eye specialist from a Central/State Government hospital or medical college.

sections.heading: Preparation priorities
paragraphs: Build preparation around the notified stages, not a generic railway-exam syllabus. For CBT-1, practise timed objective questions in arithmetic, mental ability, and Class 10-level physics, chemistry, and life sciences. For CBT-2 Part A, add basic science and engineering topics such as engineering drawing, units and measurements, work and energy, basic electricity, safety, environment education, and IT literacy.

Treat CBT-2 Part B as trade-specific qualifying preparation. ITI and trade-apprenticeship candidates are tested in their respective trade; diploma and degree candidates choose an eligible mapped trade. Use the Directorate General of Training’s official trade syllabus as the syllabus reference identified in the CEN.

Use the official RRB mock tests when released to become familiar with the computer-based interface. For CBAT, use the Railway Design and Standards Organisation’s candidate guidance and practise working accurately under the allotted time. Because candidates must clear every CBAT battery separately, preparation should address each battery rather than relying on an aggregate score.
bullets: Practise accuracy as well as speed: CBT-1 and CBT-2 have one-third negative marking for an incorrect answer.
Confirm A-1 medical eligibility before committing substantial preparation time, particularly vision requirements and the LASIK exclusion.
Keep the current CEN open while studying: question counts, cut-offs, trade lists, language options, and selection rules can be revised by a later notification.

sections.heading: Outcome after selection
paragraphs: A successful candidate can be recommended for appointment as an Assistant Loco Pilot in Indian Railways. In the CEN 01/2026 context, this was a Level 2 post with initial pay of ₹19,900 under the 7th CPC pay matrix. Actual appointment follows the Railway Zone’s offer and remains conditional on final document verification, antecedent/character verification, and passing the Railway medical examination.

This recruitment can also create a useful signalling outcome for candidates who consent in the application: the RRB notice says scores may be shared with other ministries, departments, PSUs, and private organisations for their own recruitment. That optional score-sharing provision is not an offer of employment and does not replace the ALP selection process.
bullets: An RRB’s empanelment recommendation is not itself the appointment letter; the relevant Railway Zone issues the offer.
Failing the prescribed ALP medical standard prevents empanelment for the post, and the 2026 notice says no alternative appointment is provided on that basis.
